sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian state agriculture departments, Citrus Australia and citrus industry bodies, university horticulture extension, and the WANATCA and Rare Fruit Council of Australia archives.

core.intro: Mandarins are the easiest citrus for most Australian gardens and the pick for fresh eating, with a loose skin that slips off in seconds. The basics are the same wherever you live: full sun, free-draining soil, a named grafted tree, and steady water and feeding. The state sections below cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Mandarins ripen in a sequence from autumn into spring, so the trick is to match the variety to your climate and to the season you want fruit. Imperial is the popular early variety, mild and easy to peel; the Satsuma group (Okitsu Wase, Miho Wase and Silverhill) is early, very cold hardy and reliably seedless; Emperor is a large, easy-peel favourite; Daisy, Fremont and the Clementines fill the middle of the season; and Hickson, Ellendale (a tangor), Afourer and Honey Murcott carry it late, with the big, very sweet Sumo (sold as Shiranui) latest of all. Seediness varies a lot: Hickson holds about a dozen seeds and Honey Murcott is seedy wherever it grows, while Satsuma and Pixie are close to seedless. For a single backyard tree, an early Satsuma or Imperial is the most forgiving place to start.

sections.heading: Pollination and seeds: the mandarin surprise
sections.body: Here is the quirk that catches out new growers: a single mandarin crops perfectly well on its own. Most mandarins set fruit without pollination (a trait called parthenocarpy), so you do not need a second tree as a pollinator. What another citrus nearby can change is the number of seeds. Many of the best varieties (the Clementines, Afourer and Imperial among them) are self-incompatible: grown by themselves they set few or no seeds, but when bees carry pollen to them from a different citrus, such as an orange or lemon next door, the same fruit comes out seedy. Afourer is the textbook case, seedless in a block on its own and seedy beside other citrus. So if seedless fruit matters to you, plant your mandarin away from other citrus. Navel oranges and Satsumas make very little usable pollen and are safe neighbours, while Clementines and Afourer are strong pollen donors. Honey Murcott is the exception that comes out seedy wherever it grows.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Mandarins want full sun and free-draining soil, and they will not put up with wet feet. On heavy clay, dig in gypsum and plant on a low mound so water drains away from the roots, and keep the graft union (the bulge low on the trunk) well clear of the soil so it never sits damp. Mulch generously to hold moisture and keep the roots cool, but pull the mulch back a hand's width from the trunk. Plant in the warmer months once the danger of hard frost has passed, and do not put fertiliser in the planting hole. Container-grown trees can go in through most of the year as long as you keep the water up over summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Citrus are thirsty and hungry, and mandarins reward both. Water deeply and regularly, especially through flowering and while the fruit is swelling over summer; never let a young tree dry out, but never leave it waterlogged either, because the poor drainage that drowns the roots also lets root rots in. On feeding, citrus are among the heaviest nitrogen users of any fruit tree. The home rule is little and often with a complete citrus fertiliser: about a tablespoon of nitrogen three times in the first year, building to roughly half a kilogram of actual nitrogen a year (around one pound) for a mature tree from about year five, split into a few feeds. Time the main feed just before flowering in late winter or early spring, with a second in late spring or early summer, and then ease right off, because feeding late in the season pushes soft growth, roughens the rind and delays colour. Well-rotted manure, compost and blood and bone work well alongside or instead. Mandarins are also prone to trace-element shortages that show as yellowing leaves: iron, zinc and manganese all lock up on alkaline soils, so a slightly acidic soil suits citrus best, and a complete citrus food that includes trace elements heads off most of the trouble.

sections.heading: Harvest and storage
sections.body: Citrus do not ripen any further once they are picked, so the fruit keeps best left on the tree, not in a bowl. Taste is the only reliable test, because mandarins often colour up well before they are sweet, and in warm districts ripe fruit can still look a little green. The catch with mandarins is that, unlike oranges, they do not hold once ripe: leave them too long and the skin puffs away from the flesh and the segments dry out, so pick them promptly when they taste right. Picked fruit keeps about four to six weeks in the fridge.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named variety grafted onto a citrus rootstock, not a seedling. Mandarins are not grown from seed: seedlings are thorny, take many more years to fruit and may not come true, and grafting onto a chosen rootstock also gives the tree resistance to the root rots, nematodes and tristeza virus that plague citrus on their own roots. For a pot, a courtyard or a small garden, look for a tree grafted on the dwarfing rootstock Flying Dragon, which holds most citrus under about two and a half metres without changing the fruit. Australian nurseries propagate from certified, virus-tested budwood, so a tree from a specialist citrus or fruit-tree nursery is worth the small premium. The in-stock table above shows the named mandarins available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How much does a mandarin tree cost?
faqs.a: Most mandarins sell for much the same as other citrus, with dwarf and advanced (larger, more established) trees costing more than a standard potted one. The in-stock table above lists the live price from every nursery we track, so you can compare a mail-order tree against a local pickup and see the range before you buy.

faqs.q: Can I grow a mandarin tree in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, and mandarins are one of the better citrus for it. Choose a tree grafted on a dwarfing rootstock, use a large pot of free-draining mix, and water and feed a little more often than you would in the ground, because pots dry out fast and nutrients wash straight through. Stand it in the sunniest, most sheltered spot you have, and pot it up or refresh the top of the mix every couple of years.

faqs.q: How long until a new mandarin tree starts fruiting?
faqs.a: A grafted mandarin usually carries its first real crop two to three years after planting. It pays to strip off most of the flowers and fruit in the first year or two so the young tree builds a strong frame of branches instead of ripening a handful of fruit too early.

faqs.q: Why is the flesh of my mandarins dry and grainy inside?
faqs.a: Dry, grainy segments are called granulation, and it shows up most in Imperial, in oversized fruit, and in fruit left hanging past its best, with heavy nitrogen and patchy watering making it worse. Picking as soon as the fruit tastes ripe, watering evenly and going easy on late feeds all help keep the segments juicy.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a mandarin, a tangor and a tangelo?
faqs.a: They are all easy-peel citrus. A true mandarin is its own kind; a tangor is a mandarin crossed with an orange (Ellendale is the classic Australian tangor, rich and a little tart); and a tangelo is a mandarin crossed with a grapefruit or pomelo (Minneola is the well-known one). All three are sold as mandarins, peel easily and want the same conditions.

faqs.q: Can you buy Sumo mandarin trees in Australia?
faqs.a: Yes. Sumo is the American brand name for Shiranui, a large, sweet, easy-peel Japanese variety (a Kiyomi by ponkan cross) with a distinctive bump at the stem end; the fruit is also marketed as Dekopon. Australian nurseries sell the trees under the Shiranui name, sometimes labelled Sumo or Dekopon, and several of the nurseries treestock tracks list them, including Queensland-only and Perth-local sellers, so quarantine rules decide which one can ship to you. Check the live table above for current stock, or set a variety alert and the daily digest will flag the next release.

WA.intro: Growing mandarins in Western Australia. WA grows excellent citrus and a backyard mandarin is almost a given, but the state's strict quarantine shapes everything from which trees you can buy to which pests you have to watch.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The south-west of WA has a Mediterranean climate, hot dry summers and mild wet winters, that citrus take to readily, and the warm north is warmer still. Mandarins crop well right across the settled south-west as long as they get summer water, while the subtropical Gascoyne in the north is frost free and brings its fruit on early.

sections.heading: Where mandarins grow in WA
sections.body: WA's citrus belt runs from the West Midlands down through Gingin, Bindoon, Moora and the hills to Harvey in the south-west, with smaller plantings at Manjimup and, in the warm north, Carnarvon and Kununurra. More than a thousand hectares of citrus are grown in the state, and backyard mandarins do well across the whole of that range.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA picks mandarins from about April to November. Imperial opens the season in May, and a run of later varieties then keeps fruit coming right through winter.

sections.heading: Pests in WA
sections.body: WA's main fruit-fly threat is the Mediterranean fruit fly, which is established across the state; the Queensland fruit fly of the eastern states is kept out. Citrus gall wasp is established in suburban gardens but has not reached WA's commercial orchards, so cut out any woody galls you find to help slow its spread. WA is free of citrus canker.

sections.heading: Bringing citrus into WA
sections.body: This is why so many mandarins you see online are local pickup only. To protect WA's clean citrus, live citrus plants and budwood may enter the state only if they are permitted on the WA Organism List and meet strict import conditions, usually chemical treatment and a plant-health certificate from the source state. In practice that means buying a WA-grown tree, which is what the in-stock table above is filtered to.

faqs.q: Which mandarins suit Perth and the south-west?
faqs.a: Almost any mandarin grows well in the Mediterranean south-west. For a dependable backyard tree an early Satsuma or Imperial is hard to beat, and the sandy coastal soils are fine as long as you improve them with compost, keep up a thick mulch, and feed a little more through the warm months because nutrients leach quickly from sand.

faqs.q: Are mandarins worth planting in the far north of WA?
faqs.a: Yes. The Gascoyne around Carnarvon and the country near Kununurra are warm and frost free, which suits citrus and ripens the fruit early. Keep the water up through the heat and watch for fruit fly, and your real limit up north is humidity and heat rather than cold.

QLD.intro: Growing mandarins in Queensland. Queensland is the engine room of the Australian mandarin, growing more than any other state, so nearly every variety you might want is at home here.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland's warm, frost-free climate suits mandarins from the subtropical south-east to the dry inland. The difference that matters is humidity: the wetter coast carries more fungal disease and favours the tougher, more forgiving varieties, while the drier inland districts grow clean fruit across a wider range.

sections.heading: Where mandarins grow in QLD
sections.body: The heart of the industry is the Central Burnett, around Gayndah and Mundubbera, the largest mandarin district in the country, with more plantings at Bundaberg and, in the far north, Mareeba. Queensland on its own grows roughly half of all the mandarins in Australia.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Imperial opens the Queensland season early, from about April or May, and the mid and late varieties (Ellendale, Hickson and the Murcotts) then carry picking through winter and into spring.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic here and stings ripening fruit, so home growers bait or net as the crop comes on. Citrus gall wasp, a native insect, raises woody galls on the stems. In the humid coastal districts fungal diseases are more troublesome than they are inland. Queensland also sits at the centre of Australia's citrus canker story: an outbreak at Emerald in 2004 was stamped out by 2009, and after a later northern detection was eradicated, Australia was declared free of citrus canker again in 2021, which is why citrus nursery stock is moved so carefully between states.

faqs.q: Which mandarin handles humid coastal Queensland best?
faqs.a: On the wet, humid coast lean toward the tougher varieties: Imperial and Emperor are reliable, while Murcott is better kept for drier inland gardens where it is less troubled by disease. Good air flow, full sun and a sharply drained spot matter more on the coast than almost anywhere.

faqs.q: Why are some mandarin trees sold for pickup in Queensland only?
faqs.a: Citrus is moved carefully around the country to keep diseases from spreading, and at times nurseries in a particular zone can only sell their live trees locally. If a listing is marked pickup or in-state only, that reflects the biosecurity rules of the day, not the quality of the tree.

NSW.intro: Growing mandarins in New South Wales. NSW grows a great deal of citrus, mostly in the warm, dry irrigated inland rather than on the humid coast, and a mandarin is part of nearly every backyard mix.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Mandarins do best in NSW where summers are warm and dry and winters are mild, which is why the irrigated inland grows such clean, sweet fruit. The humid coast carries more fungal disease, and the cold inland and tablelands can frost young trees, so match your variety and a sheltered spot to your district.

sections.heading: Where mandarins grow in NSW
sections.body: The Riverina, in the Murrumbidgee Irrigation Area around Griffith, Leeton and Hillston, is the largest citrus region in the country and the centre of NSW production. Citrus also grows along the Murray near Dareton and, in scattered pockets, on the central coast around Gosford.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: The inland season runs much like the rest of the country, with Imperial coming in early from autumn and the later varieties picking on through winter and into spring.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Citrus gall wasp is the pest NSW growers know best: this native insect lays into soft new stems, which swell into woody galls that sap the tree's vigour, so cut the galls out before the adults emerge in spring. Queensland fruit fly is also present and stings the fruit, so bait or net as the crop ripens.

faqs.q: Which mandarins suit the cold inland and tablelands of NSW?
faqs.a: In the frost-prone inland and tablelands, start with the cold-hardy Satsuma group and give young trees a warm, north-facing spot with frost protection for their first couple of winters. Established citrus shrug off light frost, but a young tree can be cut back hard by it.

faqs.q: Can I grow mandarins on the New South Wales coast?
faqs.a: You can. The coast is more humid, so expect a little more fungal disease and gall-wasp pressure, and pick an open, sunny, well-drained position to keep the air moving. Coastal gardens usually do better with the more forgiving early varieties than with the fussiest late ones.

VIC.intro: Growing mandarins in Victoria. Victoria is the cool end of the mandarin's range: the warm Murray-valley north grows them commercially, while in the cooler south the knack is a hardy variety in a warm corner.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The warm, dry, irrigated north of Victoria grows mandarins as well as anywhere, and the state's cool nights actually colour the fruit more deeply than the warm north of the country can. Further south, around Melbourne, citrus sit near the edge of their comfort: they crop in a warm, sheltered, sunny position but resent cold wet winters and frost.

sections.heading: Where mandarins grow in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's citrus is concentrated in the Sunraysia district on the Murray, around Mildura, Robinvale and Swan Hill, where hot dry summers and irrigation grow excellent fruit. Home gardeners grow mandarins right across the warmer parts of the state.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria ripens later than the warm north, and the payoff is colour: cool autumn nights bring on a deep skin colour that warmer districts struggle to match. The cold-hardy early Satsumas are the first mandarins ready, with the rest following on through winter.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is now present in parts of Victoria, so baiting and netting matter at ripening. Citrus gall wasp has spread south over recent decades and is now common in Melbourne gardens. The other watch-out is simply cold: protect young trees through their first frosty winters.

faqs.q: Which mandarin is best for cold, frosty parts of Victoria?
faqs.a: The Satsuma mandarins (Okitsu Wase, Miho Wase and Silverhill) are the most cold-hardy, ripen early before the worst of winter, and are reliably sweet and seedless, which makes them the standout choice for Melbourne and the frostier districts.

faqs.q: How do I protect a young mandarin from frost in a Melbourne garden?
faqs.a: Plant against a warm, north-facing wall or fence that soaks up the day's heat, drape the young tree in frost cloth on clear cold nights, and hold off pruning and late feeding so there is no soft new growth heading into winter. Watering the soil before a frosty night helps too, and trees toughen up as they age.
